prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
Brainstorming sessions

Why it’s overrated:
- Group brainstorming reliably produces fewer and less original ideas than individuals generating ideas alone and then combining them. Production blocking (only one person can talk at a time), evaluation apprehension (fear of judgment), social loafing, and anchoring on the first ideas all depress creativity.
- It creates an illusion of progress: lots of sticky notes and energy, little signal. Extroverts and senior voices dominate; riskier, better ideas stay unspoken.
- Synchronous time is expensive, and most of it is spent listening, not thinking.

When it does have value:
- As a way to align on problem framing and constraints—less for raw idea generation, more for shared understanding.

Better approach:
- “Alone together”: send a tight prompt and constraints, give people quiet time to ideate individually (brainwriting, Crazy 8s), then merge and critique.
- Separate divergence and convergence; use explicit criteria to score ideas.
- Use small groups with facilitation tools (round-robins, silent voting, time-boxed critique) to reduce dominance and anchoring.
- Capture decisions and next steps; limit the session to what truly requires real-time discussion.
